passages.title: Children's Literature
passages.source_html:
Children's Literature


A


Stories and poems aimed at children have an exceedingly long history: lullabies, for example, were sung in Roman times, and a few nursery games and rhymes are almost as ancient. Yet so far as written-down literature is concerned, while there were stories in print before 1700 that children often seized on when they had the chance, such as translation of Aesop’s fables, fairy-stories and popular ballads and romances, these were not aimed at young people in particular. Since the only genuinely child-oriented literature at this time would have been a few instructional works to help with reading and general knowledge, plus the odd Puritanical tract as an aid to morality, the only course for keen child readers was to read adult literature. This still occurs today, especially with adult thrillers or romances that include more exciting, graphic detail than is normally found in the literature for younger readers.


B


By the middle of the 18th century there were enough eager child readers, and enough parents glad to cater to this interest, for publishers to specialize in children’s books whose first aim was a pleasure rather than education or morality. In Britain, a London merchant named Thomas Boreham produced Cajanus, The Swedish Giant in 1742, while the more famous John Newbery published A Little Pretty Pocket Book in 1744. Its contents – rhymes, stories, children’s games plus a free gift (‘A ball and a pincushion’) – in many ways anticipated the similar lucky-dip contents of children’s annuals this century. It is a tribute to Newbery’s flair that he hit upon a winning formula quite so quickly, to be pirated almost immediately in America.


C


Such pleasing levity was not to last. Influenced by Rousseau, whose Emile (1762) decreed that all books for children save Robinson Crusoe were a dangerous diversion, contemporary critics saw to it that children’s literature should be instructive and uplifting. Prominent among such voices was Mrs Sarah Trimmer, whose magazine The Guardian of Education (1802) carried the first regular reviews of children’s books. It was she who condemned fairy-tales for their violence and general absurdity; her own stories, Fabulous Histories (1786) described talking animals who were always models of sense and decorum.


D


So the moral story for children was always threatened from within, given the way children have of drawing
out entertainment from the sternest moralist. But the greatest blow to the improving children’s book was to come from an unlikely source indeed: early 19th-century interest in folklore. Both nursery rhymes, selected by James Orchard Halliwell for a folklore society in 1842, and collection of fairy-stories by the scholarly Grimm brothers, swiftly translated into English in 1823, soon rocket to popularity with the young, quickly leading to new editions, each one more child-centered than the last. From now on younger children could expect stories written for their particular interest and with the needs of their own limited experience of life kept well to the force.


E


What eventually determined the reading of older children was often not the availability of special children’s
literature as such but access to books that contained characters, such as young people or animals, with whom they could more easily empathize, or action, such as exploring or fighting, that made few demands on adult maturity or understanding.


F


The final apotheosis of literary childhood as something to be protected from unpleasant reality came with the arrival in the late 1930s of child-centered best-sellers intend on entertainment as its most escapist. In Britain novelists such as Enid Blyton and Richmal Crompton described children who were always free to have the most unlikely adventures, secure in the knowledge that nothing bad could ever happen to them in the end. The fact that war broke out again during her books’ greatest popularity fails to register at all in the self-enclosed world inhabited by Enid Blyton’s young characters. The reaction against such dream-worlds was inevitable after World War II, coinciding with the growth of paperback sales, children’s libraries and a new spirit of moral and social concern. Urged on by committed publishers and progressive librarians, writers slowly began to explore new areas of interest while also shifting the settings of their plots from the middle-class world to which their chiefly adult patrons had always previously belonged.


G


The critical emphasis, during this development, has been divided. For some, the most important task was to rid children’s books of social prejudice and exclusiveness no longer found acceptable. Others concentrated more on the positive achievements of contemporary children’s literature. That writers of these works are now often recommended to the attention of adult as well as child readers echo the 19th-century belief that children’s literature can be shared by the generations, rather than being a defensive barrier between childhood and the necessary growth towards adult understanding.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14-18


Complete the table below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap.

14
DATEFEATURESAIMEXAMPLE
Before 1700Not aimed at young
children		Education and moralityPuritanical tract
By the middle of the
18th century		Collection of rhymes
and games		Read for pleasureA Little Pretty Pocket Book (exported to
)
Early 19th centuryGrowing interest in To be more
children-centered		Nursery rhymes and
Late 1930sStories of harm-free EntertainmentEnid Blyton and
Richarnal Crompton's
novels


正确答案： 14 stories##Stories  |  15 America  |  16 folklore##Folklore  |  17 fairy-stories##Fairy-Stories  |  18 adventures##Adventures
Question 14

定位关键信息：By the middle of the 18th century


对应原文： B段，Its contents – rhymes, stories, children’s games plus a free gift (‘A ball and a pincushion’) – in many ways anticipated the similar lucky-dip contents of children’s annuals this century.


解题思路： 根据提示词 "rhymes and games" 可以推测，所需填入的内容应是与这些内容相关的儿童文学特征。文中明确提到 "rhymes, stories, children’s games"，因此 "stories" 是逻辑合理的答案

Question 15

审题： 题目要求填写该书被出口到的国家。


定位关键信息： By the middle of the 18th century


对应原文： B段，It is a tribute to Newbery’s flair that he hit upon a winning formula quite so quickly, to be pirated almost immediately in America.


解题思路： 在定位的段落中提到了该书被盗版并出口到美国，结合表格中的 "exported to" 提示，很明显答案应为 "America"。


同义替换： "exported to" 与 "pirated almost immediately in" 表示相似的意思，即该书被传播到美国。

Question 16

定位关键信息：Early 19th century


对应原文： D 段，"So the moral story for children was always threatened from within, given the way children have of drawing out entertainment from the sternest moralist. But the greatest blow to the improving children’s book was to come from an unlikely source indeed: early 19th-century interest in folklore."


解题思路： 在 D 段我们找到明确的 "interest in folklore"，明确指出这一时期对民间传说的兴趣增长，说明 "folklore" 是合理的答案。

Question 17

审题： 题目要求填写 19 世纪早期儿童文学的内容，提示部分是 "Nursery rhymes and _________"。我们需要找到与 "Nursery rhymes" 并列的内容。


定位关键信息： Early 19th century，nursery rhymes


对应原文： D 段，Both nursery rhymes, selected by James Orchard Halliwell for a folklore society in 1842, and collection of fairy-stories by the scholarly Grimm brothers, swiftly translated into English in 1823, soon rocket to popularity with the young, quickly leading to new editions, each one more child-centered than the last.


解题思路： 文中提到了格林兄弟的童话故事集，与1842年由James Orchard Halliwell为一个民间传说学会挑选的两首童谣属于并列关系。说明 "fairy-stories" 是正确答案。

Question 18

定位关键信息： Late 1930s


对应原文： F 段，In Britain novelists such as Enid Blyton and Richmal Crompton described children who were always free to have the most unlikely adventures, secure in the knowledge that nothing bad could ever happen to them in the end.


解题思路： 文中提到在英国，小说家描述说，孩子们总是可以自由地进行最不可能的冒险，因为他们知道最终不会发生什么坏事。儿童在故事中自由进行冒险，符合题目中的 "harm-free"


同义替换： "unlikely adventures" 与 "harm-free adventures" 同义，强调这些冒险是没有危险的。

Questions 19-21


Look at the following people and the list of statements below. Choose correct statement in each gap.

Options (Word Bank):
A. A Wrote criticisms of children’s literature
B. B Used animals to demonstrate the absurdity of fairy tales
C. C Was not a writer originally
D. D Translated a book into English
E. E Didn’t write in the English language
Question / ParagraphABCDE
19

Thomas Boreham ________
✅ 正确答案：C

定位关键信息：Thomas Boreham


对应原文：B段，In Britain, a London merchant named Thomas Boreham produced Cajanus, The Swedish Giant in 1742, while the more famous John Newbery published A Little Pretty Pocket Book in 1744.


解题思路：Thomas Boreham最初是伦敦的一位商人，而不是作家，因此符合陈述C中的“Was not a writer originally”
20

Mrs Sarah trimmer ________
✅ 正确答案：A

定位关键信息：Mrs Sarah Trimmer


对应原文：C段，Prominent among such voices was Mrs Sarah Trimmer, whose magazine The Guardian of Education (1802) carried the first regular reviews of children’s books.


解题思路：根据原文，Sarah Trimmer编辑了一本关于儿童文学的杂志，并且发表了对儿童书籍的定期评论，符合陈述A的描述
21

Grimm Brothers________
✅ 正确答案：E

定位关键信息：Grimm Brothers


对应原文：D段，Both nursery rhymes, selected by James Orchard Halliwell for a folklore society in 1842, and collection of fairy-stories by the scholarly Grimm brothers, swiftly translated into English in 1823, soon rocket to popularity with the young, quickly leading to new editions, each one more child-centered than the last.


解题思路：Grimm Brothers的故事最初是用其他语言写成的，后来被翻译成英语，符合陈述E的描述。

Questions 22-26


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

22

Children didn’t start to read books until 1700.

正确答案： B. FALSE

22.False A段第四行。根据“1700”定位于A段第四行，可见children在1700年前是有故事书读的，而题目1700年后children才start to read与原文不符，所以答案为False。

23

Sarah Trimmer believed that children's books should set good examples.

正确答案： A. TRUE

23.True C段第三行。利用细节信息“Sarah Trimmer”定位，题目信息“set good examples'对应原文“instructive and uplifting”。题目与原文是同义表达，所以答案为True。

24

Parents were concerned about the violence in children's books.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

24.Not Given尽管在C段有提到violence in children's book，没有具体说明家长们是否关心这个问题，所以答案是Not Given。

25

An interest in the folklore changed the direction of the development of children's books.

正确答案： A. TRUE

25.True D段第二行。利用细节词“folklore”定位于D段第二行，以及该段落最后一句话提及的"from now on younger children could expect stories written for their particular interest...”这些信息都对应题目“changed the direction of the development of children's books'”。题目与原文是同义表达，所以True.

26

Today children's book writers believe their works should appeal to both children and adults.

正确答案： A. TRUE

26.True G段第四行。利用细节信息“writers'”、

“children and adults'”定位到G段第四行，题目的

“appeal to”与原文的“recommended to the attention”

是同义表达，所以答案是True。
